---
title: "Draft Model Credit Card Approval Prediction"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_notebook: default
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r}
### Load all the packages we need for data cleaning, plotting, sampling and building the models
library(dplyr)                    # data wrangling (mutate, filter, group_by etc.)
library(janitor)                  # helper functions for cleaning up data
library(ggplot2)                  # plotting
library(leaflet)                  # maps (not really needed here, but loaded anyway)
library(scales)                   # nice axis scales and the cut_number function used later
library(tidyr)                    # reshaping data (pivot_wider etc.)
library(caret)                    # machine learning helpers like upSample and confusionMatrix
library(corrplot)                 # correlation plots
library(smotefamily)              # SMOTE style sampling for imbalanced data
library(performanceEstimation)    # model performance tools
library(ROSE)                     # another sampling method for imbalanced data
library(randomForest)             # random forest model
library(glmnet)                   # ridge and lasso regression
library(pROC)                     # ROC curve and AUC score
library(e1071)                    # support vector machine (SVM)
```


```{r}
# Read the two source CSV files into R
application <- read.csv("application_record.csv")   # applicant details (income, age, job etc.)
credit<- read.csv("credit_record.csv")              # monthly payment history for each applicant
```


```{r}
# Quick look at the structure of the data
# str(application)
# str(credit)
glimpse(credit)                   # show columns, types and a few sample values from the credit data
```

### Target setting

STATUS: 0: 1-29 days past due 1: 30-59 days past due 2: 60-89 days overdue 3: 90-119 days overdue 4: 120-149 days overdue 5: Overdue or bad debts, write-offs for more than 150 days C: paid off that month X: No loan for the month.

Customer with over 30 days or more past due would be considered as a ‘bad’ customer. 
Based on this consideration, we can adjust the value of “STATUS” column to be 1 (bad customer) and 0 (good customer) and make it as our target for our model.
```{r}

# Build the target variable from the STATUS column
credit$target <- credit$STATUS          # start by copying STATUS into a new column called target
unique(credit$target)                   # check all the different values STATUS can take

# Treat "X" (no loan) and "C" (paid off) as good customers -> 0, keep the rest as they are
credit$target <- ifelse(credit$target == 'X' | credit$target == 'C', 0, credit$target)
credit$target <- as.integer(credit$target)   # turn the values into whole numbers
credit$target[credit$target >= 1] <- 1       # anything 1 or more (30+ days late) becomes a bad customer -> 1

credit_df <- aggregate(target ~ ID, data = credit, FUN = max)
```
Target Rate 
```{r}
# Count how many good (0) vs bad (1) customers we have
dplyr::count(credit_df, target, sort = TRUE)
```
Cleaning Application data
```{r}
# Fix up data types and turn some yes/no text columns into 0/1 numbers
application <- application %>% 
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor) %>%   # text columns become factors
  mutate_if(is.double, as.integer) %>%     # decimal number columns become whole numbers
  mutate(CODE_GENDER = as.integer(ifelse(CODE_GENDER == "M", 1, 0)),          # Male = 1, Female = 0
         FLAG_OWN_CAR = as.integer(ifelse(FLAG_OWN_CAR == "Y", 1, 0)),        # owns a car = 1, else 0
         FLAG_OWN_REALTY = as.integer(ifelse(FLAG_OWN_REALTY == "Y", 1, 0)))  # owns property = 1, else 0
```


```{r}
# Join the applicant details with the good/bad target using the shared ID column
credit_data <- merge(application, credit_df, by = "ID") %>% 
  select(-c(ID))                  # drop the ID column since we don't need it for modelling
head(credit_data)                 # peek at the first few rows of the combined data
```


```{r}
# Count of good vs bad customers in the merged dataset
dplyr::count(credit_data, target, sort = TRUE)
```
### Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
# See how good/bad customers break down by income type
credit_data %>%
  group_by(NAME_INCOME_TYPE, target) %>%
  summarise(Freq = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%              # count records in each group
  pivot_wider(names_from = target, values_from = Freq, values_fill = 0) %>%  # make target 0/1 into columns
  arrange(NAME_INCOME_TYPE)                                # sort by income type
```


```{r}
# Same breakdown but by education level

credit_data %>%
  group_by(NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE, target) %>%
  summarise(Freq = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = target, values_from = Freq, values_fill = 0) %>%
  arrange(NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE)
```


```{r}
# Same breakdown but by family status

credit_data %>%
  group_by(NAME_FAMILY_STATUS, target) %>%
  summarise(Freq = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = target, values_from = Freq, values_fill = 0) %>%
  arrange(NAME_FAMILY_STATUS)
```


```{r}
# Same breakdown but by housing type

credit_data %>%
  group_by(NAME_HOUSING_TYPE, target) %>%
  summarise(Freq = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = target, values_from = Freq, values_fill = 0) %>%
  arrange(NAME_HOUSING_TYPE)
```


```{r}
# Same breakdown but by occupation type

credit_data %>%
  group_by(OCCUPATION_TYPE, target) %>%
  summarise(Freq = n(),.groups = 'drop' ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = target, values_from = Freq, values_fill = 0) %>%
  arrange(OCCUPATION_TYPE)
```
### Data Preprocessing

```{r}
# Roll the many income types into three simpler groups
credit_data <- credit_data %>%
  mutate(GROUPED_INCOME_TYPE = case_when(
    NAME_INCOME_TYPE %in% c("Commercial associate", "State servant","Working") ~ "Employed",  # working people
    NAME_INCOME_TYPE %in% c("Pensioner") ~ "Retired",                                         # pensioners
    TRUE ~ "Other"                                                                            # everything else
  ))
unique(credit_data$GROUPED_INCOME_TYPE)   # check the new groups
```


```{r}
# Roll education levels into simpler groups
credit_data <- credit_data %>%
  mutate(GROUPED_EDUCATION_TYPE = case_when(
    NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE %in% c("Academic degree", "Higher education") ~ "Higher education",                
    NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE %in% c("Incomplete higher","Secondary / secondary special") ~"Secondary Special",  
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))
unique(credit_data$GROUPED_EDUCATION_TYPE)

```


```{r}
# Roll family status into simpler groups
credit_data <- credit_data %>%
  mutate(GROUPED_FAMILY_STATUS = case_when(
    NAME_FAMILY_STATUS %in% c("Civil marriage", "Married") ~ "Married",           # living with a partner
    NAME_FAMILY_STATUS %in% c("Separated", "Single / not married") ~ "Single",    # on their own
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))
unique(credit_data$GROUPED_FAMILY_STATUS)
```


```{r}
# Group the housing types into fewer categories
credit_data <- credit_data %>%
  mutate(NAME_HOUSING_TYPE = case_when(
    NAME_HOUSING_TYPE %in% c("Co-op apartment", "House / apartment", "With parents") ~ "House Apt",  # regular housing
    NAME_HOUSING_TYPE %in% c("Municipal apartment", "Office apartment") ~ "Office Apt",              # office/municipal
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))
unique(credit_data$NAME_HOUSING_TYPE)
```


```{r}
# Group the long list of jobs into a few broad categories
credit_data <- credit_data %>%
  mutate(GROUPED_OCCUPATION_TYPE = case_when(
    OCCUPATION_TYPE %in% c("Accountants", "High skill tech staff", "IT staff", "Managers") ~ "Professional",  # skilled/office roles
    OCCUPATION_TYPE %in% c("Cleaning staff", "Cooking staff", "Drivers", "Laborers", "Low-skill Laborers", "Medicine staff", "Private service staff", "Realty agents", "Sales staff", "Security staff", "Waiters/barmen staff") ~ "Service/Manual",  # hands-on/service roles
    OCCUPATION_TYPE %in% c("Core staff", "Secretaries") ~ "Support",   # support roles
    TRUE ~ "Other"                                                     # blanks and anything else
  )) 
unique(credit_data$GROUPED_OCCUPATION_TYPE)
```


```{r}
## Turn the "days" columns into easy to read ages and years worked
# The original values are negative days counted back from today, so we flip the sign and divide by days in a year
credit_data$AGE <- round(-credit_data$DAYS_BIRTH/365.24, 0)             # age in years
credit_data$YEARS_EMPLOYED <- round(-credit_data$DAYS_EMPLOYED/365.24, 0)  # years employed
```


```{r}
# Pensioners often have a strange negative "years employed" value, so fix those
negative_years_pensioner <- credit_data$YEARS_EMPLOYED < 0 & credit_data$NAME_INCOME_TYPE == "Pensioner"  # find the bad rows
credit_data$YEARS_EMPLOYED[negative_years_pensioner] <- 0                                                 # reset them to 0 first
credit_data$YEARS_EMPLOYED[negative_years_pensioner] <- credit_data$AGE[negative_years_pensioner] - 18    # estimate years worked as age minus 18 (assumed start age)

```


```{r}
## Drop the columns we no longer need (originals we already grouped, and the raw day columns)
final_df <- subset(credit_data, select = -c(NAME_INCOME_TYPE, NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE, NAME_FAMILY_STATUS, NAME_HOUSING_TYPE,OCCUPATION_TYPE,
                                            DAYS_BIRTH, DAYS_EMPLOYED,FLAG_MOBIL))
str(final_df)                          # look at the final set of columns
prop.table(table(final_df$target))     # see the proportion of good vs bad (shows the imbalance)
```

```{r}
## Standardize the income column so it's on the same scale as other features
mean_val <- mean(final_df$AMT_INCOME_TOTAL)   # average income
sd_val <- sd(final_df$AMT_INCOME_TOTAL)       # spread (standard deviation) of income

# Convert income to a z-score: how many standard deviations above/below the average
final_df$AMT_INCOME_TOTAL <- (final_df$AMT_INCOME_TOTAL - mean_val) / sd_val
```

### Train Test Split
```{r}
set.seed(80307)   # fix the random seed so the split is the same every time we run it
train_indices <- sample(x= nrow(final_df), size= nrow(final_df)* 0.70)   # randomly pick 70% of the rows for training

# Split the rows into training and testing sets
train_data <- final_df[train_indices,]    # the chosen 70%
test_data <- final_df[-train_indices,]     # the remaining 30%

# Check how many rows and columns each set has
dim(train_data)
dim(test_data)

# Check the good/bad balance in each set
table(train_data$target)
table(test_data$target)
```

```{r}
# Turn the data into the numeric matrix format that glmnet/svm need
# model.matrix builds dummy variables for the text columns; [, -1] drops the intercept column
train_x <- model.matrix(target ~ ., train_data)[, -1] 
test_x <- model.matrix(target ~ ., test_data)[,-1]

# Pull out the target column on its own
train_y <- train_data$target
test_y <- test_data$target

dim(train_x)
dim(test_x)
```

### Base Logistic Regression Model

```{r}
# Fit a basic logistic regression using every feature to predict good/bad
model_glm <- glm(target ~., data=train_data, family= binomial(link="logit"))
summary(model_glm)   # show coefficients and p-values
```

```{r}
# Get predicted probabilities on the training data and turn them into 0/1 using a 0.5 cutoff
probabilities.train_glm <- predict(model_glm, newdata=train_data, type="response")
predicted.classes.train_glm <- ifelse(probabilities.train_glm > 0.5, 1, 0)

probabilities.train_glm = as.integer(probabilities.train_glm)   # (note: this line rounds probabilities to integers)
```


```{r}
# Model accuracy
# conf_matrix_glm <- confusionMatrix(probabilities.train_glm, train_data$target)  # commented out (was throwing an error)
```


```{r}
## Check which classes the model actually predicted
unique(predicted.classes.train_glm)
```
Here we can see model is not able to predict 1 class and giving all 0 prediction

```{r}
# Try again using only the features that came out statistically significant (low p-value)
significant_features <- c("CODE_GENDER", "FLAG_OWN_CAR", "FLAG_OWN_REALTY", "AMT_INCOME_TOTAL", "FLAG_EMAIL", "GROUPED_INCOME_TYPE", "GROUPED_OCCUPATION_TYPE", "AGE", "target")

selected_model <- glm(target ~ ., family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = train_data[, significant_features])

# View the summary of the new model
summary(selected_model)
```


```{r}
# Predict with the smaller model and label as Yes/No using a 0.5 cutoff
probabilities.train_glm2 <- predict(selected_model, newdata=train_data, type="response")
predicted.classes.train_glm2 <- as.factor(ifelse(probabilities.train_glm2 >=0.5,"Yes", "No"))

# Model accuracy
# conf_matrix_glm <- confusionMatrix(probabilities.train_glm, train_data$target, positive = "Yes")
## Giving error
```


```{r}
unique(predicted.classes.train_glm2)
# Giving only "no" in prediction
```
### Ridge Regularization

```{r}
# Use cross validation to find the best lambda (penalty strength) for ridge regression
set.seed(80307)
cv.ridge <- cv.glmnet(train_x, train_y, nfolds = 10, alpha=0)   # alpha = 0 means ridge
plot(cv.ridge)   # plot error against lambda
```


```{r}
# Print the two useful lambda values from cross validation
cat("lambda.min:", cv.ridge$lambda.min, "\n")   # lambda with the lowest error
cat("lambda.1se:", cv.ridge$lambda.1se, "\n")   # slightly stronger penalty, still within 1 std error
```


```{r}
# Fit the ridge model using the best (lowest error) lambda
model.min_ridge <- glmnet(train_x, train_y, alpha= 0, lambda = cv.ridge$lambda.min)
model.min_ridge
# show the fitted coefficients
coef(model.min_ridge)
summary(model.min_ridge)
```


```{r}
# Predict on the test set and turn probabilities into 0/1
pred_probs <- predict(model.min_ridge, newx = test_x, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Build a confusion matrix comparing actual vs predicted
conf_matrix <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = pred_class)
conf_matrix
```


```{r}
# Accuracy = correct predictions (diagonal) divided by total predictions
accuracy_ridge <- sum(diag(conf_matrix)) / sum(conf_matrix)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_ridge, "\n")
```
Unable to calculate recall because we don't have FP and TP 
Still after ridge regularization model is not able to predict class 1

### Lasso Regularization
```{r}
# Same idea as ridge, but now find the best lambda for lasso regression
set.seed(80307)
cv.lasso <- cv.glmnet(train_x, train_y, nfolds = 10, alpha=1)   # alpha = 1 means lasso
plot(cv.lasso)
```


```{r}
# Print the best lambda values for lasso
cat("lambda.min_lasso:", cv.lasso$lambda.min, "\n")
cat("lambda.1se_lasso:", cv.lasso$lambda.1se, "\n")
```

```{r}
# Fit the lasso model with the best lambda
model.min_lasso <- glmnet(train_x, train_y, alpha= 1, lambda = cv.lasso$lambda.min)
model.min_lasso
# show the coefficients (lasso can push some all the way to zero)
coef(model.min_lasso)
```


```{r}
# Predict on the test set and score with a confusion matrix
pred_probs <- predict(model.min_lasso, newx = test_x, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)
conf_matrix_lasso <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = pred_class)
conf_matrix_lasso
```

```{r}
# Accuracy for the lasso model
accuracy_lasso <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_lasso)) / sum(conf_matrix_lasso)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_lasso, "\n")
```

### Support Vector Machine model
```{r}
# Train an SVM with a radial kernel, then predict on the training data
svm_model <- svm(x = train_x, y = train_y, kernel = "radial", cost = 0.1)
predictions_svm <- predict(svm_model, newdata = train_x)
predicted.classes.svm <- ifelse(predictions_svm >=0.5,1,0)   # convert to 0/1 labels
```


```{r}
# Confusion matrix and accuracy for the SVM on training data
conf_matrix_svm <- table(Actual = train_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.svm)
accuracy_svm <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_svm)) / sum(conf_matrix_svm)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_svm, "\n")
```
```{r}
conf_matrix_svm
```
SVM model is also not able to predict class 1

### Sampling
```{r}
# The data is heavily imbalanced, so first cut down the good customers (target = 0) to even things out
train_data$target <- as.integer(train_data$target)

df_0 <- train_data %>% 
  filter(target == 0) %>%      # keep only good customers
  slice(-c(1:10000))           # drop the first 10,000 of them to reduce the majority class

df_1 <- train_data %>% 
  filter(target == 1)          # keep all the bad customers
```


```{r}
# Stick the reduced good customers and all bad customers back together
credit_train <- rbind(df_0, df_1)
dim(credit_train)
```

#### Applying Upsample technique on training dataset
```{r}

credit_train$target <- as.factor(credit_train$target)   # upSample needs the target as a factor

# Up-sample the minority class so both classes have equal counts
ups_df <- upSample(x = credit_train %>% select(-target),
                   y = credit_train$target,
                   yname = "target")
table(ups_df$target)   # confirm both classes are now balanced
```

#### Prepare data for modelling 
```{r}
# Rebuild the numeric training matrix and target from the balanced data
train_x <- model.matrix(target ~ ., ups_df)[, -1]
train_y <- ups_df$target
dim(train_x)
dim(test_x)
```


```{r}
# Compare test set balance vs the new balanced training set
table(test_data$target)
table(ups_df$target)
```
We can see Now training target is upsample and it's no more imbalanced dataset

### Logistic Regression 
```{r}
# Fit logistic regression on the balanced training data
model <- glm(target ~., data=ups_df, family= binomial())
summary(model)
```


```{r}
# Predict on the test data, convert to 0/1, then build a confusion matrix
probabilities.test <- predict(model, newdata=test_data, type="response")
predicted.classes.test <- ifelse(probabilities.test>=0.5,1,0)
test_y <- as.factor(as.character(test_y))                    # make sure actual and predicted are the same type
predicted.classes.test <- as.factor(predicted.classes.test)
conf_matrix_lr <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.test)
conf_matrix_lr
```

```{r}
# Accuracy and recall for the balanced logistic regression
accuracy_lr <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_lr)) / sum(conf_matrix_lr)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_lr, "\n")

# Recall = of all the actual bad customers, how many did we catch
recall_lr <- conf_matrix_lr[2, 2] / sum(conf_matrix_lr[2, ])
cat("Recall:", recall_lr, "\n")
```

### Ridge Regularization
```{r}
# Find the best ridge lambda again, this time on the balanced data
train_y = as.integer(as.character(train_y))   # glmnet needs a numeric target
set.seed(80307)
cv.ridge_reg <- cv.glmnet(train_x, train_y, nfolds = 10, alpha=0)
plot(cv.ridge_reg)
```

```{r}
# Print the best lambda values
cat("lambda.min:", cv.ridge_reg$lambda.min, "\n")
cat("lambda.1se:", cv.ridge_reg$lambda.1se, "\n")
```


```{r}
# Fit the ridge model on balanced data using the best lambda
model.min_ridge_reg <- glmnet(train_x, train_y, alpha= 0, lambda = cv.ridge_reg$lambda.min)

coef(model.min_ridge_reg)
summary(model.min_ridge_reg)
```


```{r}
# Predict on test data and build the confusion matrix
pred_probs <- predict(model.min_ridge_reg, newx = test_x, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)

conf_matrix_ridge_reg <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = pred_class)
conf_matrix_ridge_reg
```


```{r}
# Accuracy and recall for the balanced ridge model
accuracy_ridge <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_ridge_reg)) / sum(conf_matrix_ridge_reg)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_ridge, "\n")

recall_ridge <- conf_matrix_ridge_reg[2, 2] / sum(conf_matrix_ridge_reg[2, ])
cat("Recall:", accuracy_ridge, "\n")
```

### Lasso Regularization
```{r}
# Find the best lasso lambda on the balanced data
set.seed(80307)
cv.lasso_reg <- cv.glmnet(train_x, train_y, nfolds = 10, alpha=1)
plot(cv.lasso_reg)

```
```{r}
# Print the best lasso lambda values
cat("lambda.min_lasso:", cv.lasso_reg$lambda.min, "\n")
cat("lambda.1se_lasso:", cv.lasso_reg$lambda.1se, "\n")
```

```{r}
# Fit the lasso model with the best lambda
model.min_lasso_reg <- glmnet(train_x, train_y, alpha= 1, lambda = cv.lasso_reg$lambda.min)
model.min_lasso_reg
# show the coefficients
coef(model.min_lasso_reg)
```

```{r}
# Predict on test data and build the confusion matrix
pred_probs <- predict(model.min_lasso_reg, newx = test_x, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)

conf_matrix_lasso_reg <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = pred_class)
conf_matrix_lasso_reg
```

```{r}
# Accuracy and recall for the balanced lasso model
accuracy_lasso <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_lasso_reg)) / sum(conf_matrix_lasso_reg)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_lasso, "\n")

recall_lasso <- conf_matrix_lasso_reg[2, 2] / sum(conf_matrix_lasso_reg[2, ])
cat("Recall:", recall_lasso, "\n")
```

### Support Vector Machine model
```{r}
# Train an SVM on the balanced data and check it on the training set
svm_model <- svm(x = train_x, y = train_y, kernel = "radial", cost = 0.1)
predictions_svm <- predict(svm_model, newdata = train_x)
predicted.classes.svm <- ifelse(predictions_svm >=0.5,1,0)
conf_matrix_svm <- table(Actual = train_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.svm)
conf_matrix_svm
```


```{r}
# Training accuracy and recall for the SVM
accuracy_svm <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_svm)) / sum(conf_matrix_svm)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_svm, "\n")

recall_svm <- conf_matrix_svm[2, 2] / sum(conf_matrix_svm[2, ])
cat("Recall:", recall_svm, "\n")
```


```{r}
#### Now check the SVM on the unseen test data
predictions_svm_test <- predict(svm_model, newdata = test_x)
predicted.classes.svm_test <- ifelse(predictions_svm_test >=0.5,1,0)
conf_matrix_svm_test <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.svm_test)
conf_matrix_svm_test
```


```{r}
# Test accuracy and recall for the SVM
accuracy_svm <- sum(diag(conf_matrix_svm_test)) / sum(conf_matrix_svm_test)
cat("Accuracy:", accuracy_svm, "\n")

recall_svm <- conf_matrix_svm_test[2, 2] / sum(conf_matrix_svm_test[2, ])
cat("Recall:", recall_svm, "\n")
```


```{r}
# ROC AUC Score: measures how well the model separates good from bad across all cutoffs
probabilities <- predict(svm_model, test_x, probability = TRUE)

roc_curve <- roc(test_y, probabilities)   # build the ROC curve
svm_roc_auc <- auc(roc_curve)             # area under the curve (closer to 1 is better)
print(svm_roc_auc)
```


```{r}
# Draw the ROC curve
plot(roc_curve, main = "ROC AUC Curve for SVM", col = "blue")
# Add the AUC number to the plot as a legend
legend("bottomright", legend = paste("AUC =", round(auc(roc_curve), 2)), col = "blue", lty = 1, cex = 0.8)

```
### Random Forest Classifier

```{r}
# Train a random forest with 100 trees on the balanced data
rf_model <- randomForest(x = train_x, y = train_y, ntree = 100) #probabilities=TRUE)

```


```{r}
# Predict on the training data and score it
predictions_train <- predict(rf_model,train_x)
predicted.classes.rf <- ifelse(predictions_train >=0.5,1,0)
confusion_matrix_rf_tt <- table(Actual = train_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.rf)
confusion_matrix_rf_tt
```


```{r}
# Predict on the test data and score it
predictions_test <- predict(rf_model, test_x)
predicted.classes.rf_test <- ifelse(predictions_test >=0.5,1,0)
confusion_matrix_rf_test <- table(Actual = test_y, Predicted = predicted.classes.rf_test)
confusion_matrix_rf_test
```

```{r}
# Recall on the test set: how many actual bad customers the forest caught
recall_rf_test <- confusion_matrix_rf_test[2, 2] / sum(confusion_matrix_rf_test[2, ])
cat("Recall:", recall_rf_test, "\n")
```


```{r}
# ROC AUC Score for the random forest
#probabilities_rf <- predict(rf_model, test_x, probability = TRUE)

rf_roc_curve <- roc(test_y, predictions_test)
rf_roc_auc <- auc(roc_curve)
print(rf_roc_auc)

```

```{r}
## Pull out how important each feature was in the random forest
feat_imp_df <- importance(rf_model) %>% 
  data.frame() %>% 
  mutate(feature = row.names(.))   # turn the row names into a proper feature column
```


```{r}
# Plot the feature importance as a horizontal bar chart (most important on top)
ggplot(feat_imp_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, IncNodePurity), 
                        y = IncNodePurity)) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity') +
  coord_flip() +                 # flip so labels are readable
  theme_classic() +
  labs(
    x     = "Feature",
    y     = "Importance",
    title = "Feature Importance: <Model>"
  )

```

### Decile mapping
```{r}
# Attach the predictions back to the test data so we can rank customers by risk
mapped_test_x <- data.frame(test_x, 
                            PredictedProb = 1- predictions_test,   # flip so higher = more likely good (or as intended)
                            prediction = predicted.classes.rf_test,
                            actual_target= test_y)
dim(mapped_test_x)
str(mapped_test_x)

# Split customers into 10 equal groups (deciles) based on their predicted probability
mapped_test_x$decile <- as.numeric(cut_number(mapped_test_x$PredictedProb,10))
unique(mapped_test_x$decile)
table(mapped_test_x$decile)
```


```{r}
# Save the scored and decile-ranked test data to a CSV file
write.csv(mapped_test_x, "decile_output2.csv", row.names=TRUE)
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```


```{r}
```

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press *Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.
